

### Paste in a template
---
title: "Client Report - [Names]"
subtitle: "Coding challange"
author: "[Shegbe Koker]"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

_THIS `.qmd` IS INSTRUCTIONAL AND SHOULD `NOT` BE USED TO WRITE YOUR REPORTS (EXCEPTION - PROJECT 0). THERE IS ANOTHER TEMPLATE FILE FOR THAT. YOU WILL NEED TO `PREVIEW` THE REPORT TO PRODUCE A `.html` FILE. YOU WILL SUBMIT THE `.html` FILE ON CANVAS._

```{python}
#| label: libraries
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


## Elevator pitch
_A SHORT (2-3 SENTENCES) PARAGRAPH THAT `DESCRIBES KEY INSIGHTS` TAKEN FROM METRICS IN THE PROJECT RESULTS THINK TOP OR MOST IMPORTANT RESULTS._ (Note: this is not a summary of the project, but a summary of the results.)

_A Client has requested this analysis and this is your one shot of what you would say to your boss in a 2 min elevator ride before he takes your report and hands it to the client._

```{python}
#| label: project-data
#| code-summary: Read and format project data


df = pd.read_csv("https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4names/raw/master/data-raw/names_year/names_year.csv")
```

__Highlight the Questions and Tasks__

## Question|Task 1

__COPY PASTE QUESTION|TASK 1 FROM THE PROJECT HERE__

Calculate the mean after replacing the missing values with the median (2 decimal places):
```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Create the Series
probLem = pd.Series([np.nan, 15, 22, 45, 31, np.nan, 85, 38, 129, 8000, 21, 21])

# Calculate the median (ignoring NaN values)
median_value = probLem.median()

# Replace NaN values with the median
probLem_filled = probLem.fillna(median_value)

# Calculate the mean of the updated Series
mean_value = probLem_filled.mean()

# Print the mean rounded to 2 decimal places
print(round(mean_value, 2))

```


```{python}
# Inspect the dataset
print(df.head())  # View the first few rows
print(df.info())  # Check the structure and data types
print(df.describe())  # Get a statistical summary of numerical columns

```

```{python}
print(df.shape)

```

```{python}
# Filter the DataFrame for the name 'Oliver'
oliver_utah = df[df['name'] == 'Oliver']

# Sum the counts for Utah (column 'UT')
total_oliver_utah = oliver_utah['UT'].sum()

print(total_oliver_utah)


# Filter the DataFrame for the name 'Felisha'
felisha_data = df[df['name'] == 'Felisha']

# Find the earliest year
earliest_year_felisha = felisha_data['year'].min()

print(earliest_year_felisha)


```

```{python}
# Filter the DataFrame for the name 'Israel'
israel_data = df[df['name'] == 'Israel']

israel_birth_year = israel_data[israel_data['year'] == 2005]

# Get the total number of occurrences in the birth year
birth_year_total = israel_birth_year['Total'].sum()

print(f"Total occurrences of the name 'Israel' in 2005: {birth_year_total}")

```

## The chart shows the historical trend of the name "Israel," with a red dashed line marking 2005. In 2005, the name "Israel" was used 1,435.5 times across the U.S. This usage is relatively high compared to previous decades, indicating that "Israel" gained popularity in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Historically, the name has shown steady growth, with occasional fluctuations over time.


```{python}
# Filter data for the name 'Israel'
israel_data = df[df['name'] == 'Israel']

# Group by year and sum the total occurrences
israel_trend = israel_data.groupby('year')['Total'].sum().reset_index()

# Plot the historical trend
ggplot(israel_trend, aes(x='year', y='Total')) + \
    geom_line(color='blue') + \
    geom_vline(xintercept=2005, linetype='dashed', color='red', size=1) + \
    ggtitle("Historical Trend of the Name 'Israel'") + \
    xlab("Year") + ylab("Total Count")


```



## Question|Task 2

__COPY PASTE QUESTION|TASK 2 FROM THE PROJECT HERE__
If you talked to someone named Brittany on the phone, what is your guess of his or her age? What ages would you not guess?

```{python}
# | label: Q1-chart
# | code-summary: plot example
# | fig-cap: "My useless chart"
# | fig-align: center
# Include and execute your code here

#import pandas as pd


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *


Brittany_df = df[df['name']=="Brittany"]

(
  ggplot(Brittany_df, aes(x='year', y='Total')) + geom_line()+geom_vline(xintercept=1990, colour="blue")
)

```

## Question|Task 3

__COPY PASTE QUESTION|TASK 3 FROM THE PROJECT HERE__

```{python}
#| label: Q1-table
#| code-summary: table example
#| tbl-cap: "table example"
#| tbl-cap-location: top
# Include and execute your code here
mydat = (df.loc[
  (df["name"].isin(["Mary","Martha","Peter", "Paul"]))&
  (df["year"] >=1920) &
  (df["year"]<=2000),
])
(ggplot(mydat, aes(x='year', y='Total', color="name")) + geom_line())

# display(mydat)

```



##Analysis & Interpretation:
The red dashed line marks 2013, the release year of Frozen.
Before 2013, the name Elsa had relatively low usage.
After 2013, there is likely a sharp increase in popularity due to the influence of Frozen.
This shows a clear movie-driven effect on baby name trends!
```{python}
# Filter the DataFrame for the name 'Elsa'
Elsa_df = df[df['name'] == "Elsa"]

# Plot the trend
(
    ggplot(Elsa_df, aes(x='year', y='Total')) + 
    geom_line(color='cyan', size=1) + 
    geom_vline(xintercept=2013, colour="red", linetype='dashed', size=1) +  # Mark the release of Frozen
    ggtitle("Impact of 'Frozen' on the Name 'Elsa'") + 
    xlab("Year") + 
    ylab("Total Count")
)


```


```{python}
from lets_plot import *
import pandas as pd

# Ensure Lets-Plot is set up
LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

# Filter data for the name 'Elliot'
elliot_df = df[df['name'] == "Elliot"]

# Create the plot
(
    ggplot(elliot_df, aes(x='year', y='Total')) +
    geom_line(color='blue', size=1) +  # Blue trend line
    geom_vline(xintercept=1982, colour="red", linetype='dashed', size=1) +  # E.T Released
    geom_vline(xintercept=1985, colour="red", linetype='dashed', size=1) +  # Second Release
    geom_vline(xintercept=2002, colour="red", linetype='dashed', size=1) +  # Third Release
    ggtitle("Elliot... What?") + 
    xlab("Year") + 
    ylab("Total") + 
    theme_minimal()
)

```
